---
title: "Case Study 2: Technical Presentation"
subtitle: "STAT 946 – Case Studies in Data Science"
author:
  - "Dario Greco"
  - "Lovera Lokeswara"
  - "Sanika Poojary"
  - "Sophia Feng"
date: "March 9, 2026"
format:
  beamer:
    theme: Madrid
    colortheme: dolphin
    aspectratio: 169
    fontsize: 10pt
    incremental: false
    number-sections: false
    pdf-engine: pdflatex
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
header-includes:
  - \usepackage{amsmath,amssymb}
  - \usepackage{booktabs}
  - \usepackage{siunitx}
  - \newcommand{\E}{\mathbb{E}}
  - \newcommand{\Var}{\mathrm{Var}}
---

# Complex model: N-HiTS

## Why N-HiTS

Goal:

Capture nonlinearities and multi-scale temporal structure with a deep, computationally intensive model.

High-level:

- learns temporal patterns at multiple resolutions
- better suited for spike behavior and nonlinearity than linear baselines

Forecasting abstraction:

$$
\hat{y}_{t+h} = f_\theta(X_t)
$$

where $f_\theta$ is N-HiTS.

---

## N-HiTS implementation details to present

Minimum technical details to include:

- input window length $L$
- forecast horizon output (single horizon or multi-horizon head)
- block structure:
  - number of stacks
  - number of blocks per stack
  - basis functions / interpolation approach (as used in your code)
- loss function (MSE, MAE, or quantile loss if probabilistic)
- regularization (dropout, weight decay, early stopping)

Compute:

- GPU training (Colab), runtime per configuration

---

## Fine-tuning N-HiTS (grid search)

Grid search over:

- $L \in \{6,12,24,72\}$
- learning rate
- batch size
- number of blocks / hidden width
- dropout, weight decay
- early stopping patience

Selection criterion:

- validation RMSE for each horizon
- select best model per horizon (or one model overall, state which)
